title: Investigating mechanisms of tendon damage by measuring multi-scale recovery following tensile loading
abstract: Tendon pathology is associated with damage. While tendon damage is likely initiated by mechanical loading, little is known about the specific etiology. Damage is defined as an irreversible change in the microstructure that alters the macroscopic mechanical parameters. In tendon, the link between mechanical loading and microstructural damage, resulting in macroscopic changes, is not fully elucidated. In addition, tendon damage at the macroscale has been proposed to initiate when tendon is loaded beyond a strain threshold, yet the metrics to define the damage threshold are not determined. We conducted multi-scale mechanical testing to investigate the mechanism of tendon damage by simultaneously quantifying macroscale mechanical and microstructural changes. At the microscale, we observe full recovery of the fibril strain and only partial recovery of the interfibrillar sliding, indicating that the damage initiates at the interfibrillar structures. We show that non-recoverable sliding is a mechanism for tendon damage and is responsible for the macroscale decreased linear modulus and elongated toe-region observed at the fascicle-level, and these macroscale properties are appropriate metrics that reflect tendon damage. We concluded that the inflection point of the stress-strain curve represents the damage threshold and, therefore, may be a useful parameter for future studies. Establishing the mechanism of damage at multiple length scales can improve prevention and rehabilitation strategies for tendon pathology.
results: At the fascicle-level, tendon experienced a permanent change in mechanical parameters (i.e., macroscale observation of damage) that was strain-dependent. For REST groups, the Δ transition strain increased with applied tissue strain and the Δ linear region modulus decreased with applied tissue strain. The Δ inflection point strain and Δ inflection point stress were also dependent on the applied tissue strain. These results show that damage is strain dependent. The fascicle-level parameters that are strain-dependent, including the Δ transition strain, linear region modulus, inflection point strain, and inflection point stress, may be used as macro-scale metrics to quantify damage. The x-intercept of the Δ linear region modulus was 0.021, and the x-intercept of the Δ inflection point strain and stress was 0.017, suggesting that the inflection point coincides with the damage threshold at the fascicle-level.
discussion: This study quantified tendon damage, an irreversible change in the microstructure that alters the macroscopic mechanical parameters, by identifying the microstructural fibril-level changes that correlated with altered macroscopic mechanical parameters at the fascicle-level. Analysis of the fibril-level microscale deformations demonstrated that the fibril strain fully recovered following loading and unloading. However, interfibrillar sliding was only partially recoverable, suggesting tendon damage is localized to the interfibrillar structures. At the fascicle-level, irreversible mechanical parameter alterations included a strain-dependent increased transition strain, decreased linear modulus, and increased inflection point strain. In addition, we demonstrated that the inflection point of the stress-strain curve represents the damage threshold and therefore, may be a useful parameter for future studies.
conclusion: This study used multi-scale mechanics to experimentally elucidate tendon damage mechanisms, where damage was defined as an irreversible change in the microstructure that alters the macroscopic mechanical parameters. Key advances of this study were that damage was measured as non-recoverable change in mechanical behavior and the threshold for damage initiation was determined. In contrast to the observed full recovery of the load bearing collagen fibrils, the interfibrillar sliding only partially recovered, indicating that the damage was localized to the interfibrillar structures. We concluded that non-recoverable sliding is a mechanism for tendon damage and is responsible for the decreased linear modulus and elongated toe-region observed at the fascicle-level. These fascicle level changes in transition strain and linear region modulus are therefore appropriate metrics of tendon damage.
